post_text:
Everyone on LinkedIn keeps telling me to niche down.
They are wrong, and I have 18 months of client data to prove it.

The script goes: pick a vertical, own it, charge premium rates.
Sounds clean. Falls apart on contact with reality.

I watched three "AI for legal tech" consultants lose 70% of their revenue in six weeks.
One bar association put out a cautious guidance memo. That was all it took.
No other lane. Pipeline one vertical deep, and that vertical blinked.

The generalist does not have this problem. When legal slows, I rotate to e-commerce. When e-commerce slows, B2B SaaS.
Skills are skills — architecting agents, wiring RAG, picking models. They transfer with two days of domain reading.

Be a generalist who writes vertical-specific case studies. You get the specialist's proof and the generalist's pipeline resilience.

LinkedIn positioning reads AI Generalist. Portfolio page carries detailed vertical case studies.
Prospects see the generalist framing first, then find the case study that matches their problem.

The reason this take never goes viral is that it is hard to package as a $500 course.
"Niche down" is a clean template. "Be a generalist with vertical proof" is messy to teach.

Which vertical did you niche into that is now quietly squeezing your pipeline?
link_comment: The case-study portfolio structure that gets me specialist-level proof with generalist-level pipeline: https://alisadikinma.com/blog/ai-generalist-vs-specialist-2026
